title: The practice of choosing ease when difficulty is what you know
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The body that knows only one way

I spent thirty years believing that difficulty was the only real option. That ease was something other people got. Something I hadn't earned yet. Something that would arrive after I fixed everything that was wrong with me.

Here's the thing. That day never came.

My nervous system had learned one language and one language only: vigilance. Hyperarousal. The constant scanning for threat that kept me alive in a childhood that required it. And when you live in that state long enough, your body forgets there's another gear. It forgets that ease is even possible. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would call this a nervous system locked in sympathetic activation. The gas pedal stuck to the floor. No brake in sight.

I didn't know I was doing it. I thought that tightness in my chest was just how life felt. I thought the constant mental chatter, the planning, the rehearsing of conversations that would never happen, was just who I was. A diligent person. A responsible person. Someone who cared enough to worry.

But here's what I've come to understand: when your nervous system has been shaped by trauma, difficulty becomes your baseline. It becomes the water you swim in. You don't notice it because you've never known anything else. Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body as unfinished survival responses. And those unfinished responses keep your system in a state of readiness. Always preparing for a threat that already happened. Years ago. Decades ago.

Choosing ease isn't lazy. It isn't a luxury. It's a repatterning of your entire nervous system. And it's one of the hardest things you'll ever do.

The fear of letting go

I remember the first time someone told me I could just rest. I laughed. Not because I thought they were wrong, but because the idea felt so foreign it was almost comical. Rest? In the middle of all this? While there are bills to pay and emails to answer and relationships to manage and a world that keeps demanding more?

But underneath that laughter was something else. Fear. Genuine, body-level terror.

Because if I stopped, if I let myself feel ease even for a moment, what would happen? Would everything fall apart? Would the vigilance I'd relied on for so long fail me when I actually needed it? Would I miss something important, something dangerous, because I wasn't paying attention?

This is the paradox of complex trauma. The very system that kept you alive is now the system that keeps you stuck. Your body learned that hypervigilance equals safety. And anything that feels different, anything that feels like ease, gets flagged as a threat. Your nervous system doesn't know the difference between a real tiger and a memory of one. It just knows danger. And it will do everything it can to pull you back into what it knows.

Stay with me here. This isn't about willpower. It isn't about just deciding to relax. It's about teaching your body, slowly and gently, that ease is safe. That you can let your guard down without being destroyed.

I started small. Really small. Like, three minutes of sitting on my couch with my hands in my lap small. No phone. No book. No music. Just me and the discomfort of not doing anything. And my body screamed at me. My chest tightened. My mind raced. I felt like I was going to jump out of my skin.

But I stayed. Three minutes. Then five. Then ten. Over weeks, months, my system started to learn something new. That it could tolerate ease. That the world didn't end when I stopped running.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on the freeze and fawn responses helped me understand why my body clung to difficulty like a lifeline. Because for many of us, surviving meant staying small, staying busy, staying invisible. And choosing ease feels like a betrayal of that survival strategy.

The permission to want something different

Here's where it gets tricky. The wellness industry has turned "self-care" into another productivity metric. Another thing to . Another way to prove you're doing recovery right. And that's not ease. That's just difficulty wearing a different mask.

True ease isn't something you achieve. It's something you allow. Something you return to. Something your body already knows how to do, underneath all that armor.

I'm not talking about lying on a beach in Bali. I'm talking about the simple act of choosing the less effortful path when you have the option. Taking the elevator instead of the stairs because your body is tired. Ordering takeout instead of cooking because your brain is fried. Saying no to a social event because you need to be alone with your own breath.

And yes, this will feel wrong at first. It will feel selfish. Lazy. Irresponsible. Your inner critic will have a field day. But that inner critic? It's not the truth. It's a part of you that learned to keep you safe by keeping you small. By keeping you in difficulty.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz's Internal Family Systems model helped me see that the part of me that resisted ease wasn't my enemy. It was a protector. A part that believed it was keeping me alive. And once I started talking to that part with curiosity instead of shame, something shifted.

I started giving myself permission to want ease. To admit that I was exhausted. To admit that I didn't want to fight anymore. To admit that I wanted to feel good, not just functional.

That admission was terrifying. And it was also the beginning of something real.

The practice of coming home

Choosing ease is not a one-time decision. It's a daily practice. An hourly practice. Sometimes a minute-by-minute practice. Because your nervous system will keep trying to pull you back into the familiar. The familiar is not always comfortable, but it is known. And known feels safe to a traumatized brain.

So you practice. You notice when your shoulders are up by your ears and you lower them. You notice when your breath is shallow and you let it deepen. You notice when you're rushing through a task that doesn't need to be rushed and you slow down. You notice when you're bracing for disaster and you remind your body that right now, in this moment, you are safe.

This is not toxic positivity. I'm not telling you to pretend everything is fine. I'm telling you to let your body know that it's okay to not be in fight or flight mode all the time. That the threat has passed. That you are an adult now with resources and choices you didn't have then.

Dan Siegel, the clinical psychiatrist who developed the concept of integration, talks about how healing happens when we connect different parts of our experience. When we let the knowing part of our brain talk to the feeling part of our body. When we integrate the past with the present and see that they are not the same.

Right? The past is over. The threat is gone. But your body doesn't know that yet. So you have to show it. Over and over. With patience and gentleness and no small amount of grace.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). Deb Dana's work on co-regulation and nervous system mapping gave me concrete tools for noticing when I was drifting back into difficulty and gently guiding myself back toward ease. Not by force. By invitation.

What ease actually feels like

I used to think ease would feel like a weight lifting. A lightness. A freedom from all the pressure I carried.

It doesn't. At least not at first.

At first, ease feels like discomfort. Like your skin is too tight. Like you're forgetting something important. Like you're doing something wrong.

That's normal. That's your nervous system saying, "Hey, this is new. Are we sure about this?"

But if you stay with it, if you let your body adjust at its own pace, something shifts. The discomfort starts to fade. And what's left is not the explosive relief I expected. It's something quieter. A softening. A settling. A sense that you can put down the armor for a minute and nothing terrible will happen.

I'm not going to tell you that choosing ease will fix everything. It won't. The world is still hard. Life is still complicated. You will still have days when difficulty is the only option you have.

But on the days when you do have a choice, when you can pick the path that requires less effort, less vigilance, less holding on, I hope you take it. Not because you're weak. Because you're learning. Because you're teaching your body a new language. Because you deserve to know what it feels like to not be fighting all the time.

I'm still learning. I still catch myself bracing for a blow that isn't coming. I still choose difficulty sometimes because it's easier than trusting that ease is safe. But I'm getting better. And so are you.

That's the practice. Not perfection. Not arrival. Just the slow, messy, beautiful work of coming home to a body that finally knows it can rest.

Healing Journey Resources

  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate
  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
